rulings.Case Title: BRADSHAW & ASSOCIATES PC VS. SHEILA DALTON et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn To Compel Production Of Documents, For Contempt, And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Bloom'S Saloon
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Thursday, June 12, 2014, Line 1, PLAINTIFF BRADSHAW & ASSOCIATES PC's Motion To Compel Production Of Documents, For Contempt, And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Bloom's Saloon. Pro Tem Judge Nils Rosenquest, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion denied. Moving papers indicate that defendant, Sheila Dalton, is currently in bankruptcy. (1) On the motion to compel the Earnings Withholding Order and for contempt: Unless moving party has obtained relief from the stay, the earnings withholding order is barred by the automatic stay in bankruptcy. See 11 USC Section 362. Secondly, if relief from the stay has been granted, enforcement of the order is accomplished by a separate civil proceeding against the employer under CCP Section 706.154. (2) On the subpoena duces tecum: the stay may also bar post judgment discovery in state court. Moving party's remedy, absent relief from the stay, is to conduct a Rule 2004 examination or other discovery in the bankruptcy proceedings. Contempt is not available by way of a motion to compel against the third party deponent. Contempt proceedings must be prosecuted pursuant to CCP Sections 1209, et seq. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rosenquest@earthlink.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: FRANK ALIOTO et al VS. ADAM BURGESS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, June 12, 2014, Line 1, DEFENDANTS ADAM BURGESS AND LINDSAY BURGESS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT/Adjudication The motion for summary judgment and summary adjudication is denied in its entirety. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding where defendants are domiciled and the determination of that factual issue (by special verdict or otherwise) will largely dictate whether California or Mexican law applies to this case. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding the existence of a dangerous condition and causation. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether defendants exercised control over the dangerous condition. The court overrules defendants' objections to the Brault declaration. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether plaintiff Frank Alioto assumed the risk. Plaintiff's evidence demonstrates that the defendants may have increased the risk of swimming/diving to plaintiff over and above those inherent in the sport. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: BRYAN LOGAN VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn For Reconsideration Of Mtn For Summ Judgmnt; Notice Of Lodgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, June 12, 2014, Line 2, PART I OF TENTATIVE RULING, PLAINTIFF BRYAN LOGAN's motion For Reconsideration Of Motion For Summary Judgment; The motion for reconsideration is granted. Pursuant to Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal.4th 1094, the Court vacates the February 21, 2014 Order and corrects it as follows: Defendant's motion for summary judgment is granted. The Court did not consider the new evidence submitted in reply. Plaintiff did not establish a peculiar dangerous condition that would require Defendant to light the intersection. (Mixon v. PG&E ((2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 124, 133.) Design immunity shields the City from any liability arising from the placement of the limit line or pedestrian signal pole. (Govt. Code. sec. 830.6.) Sign immunity shields the City from any liability arising from lack of signage at the intersection. (Govt. Code secs. 830.4 and 830.8.) Plaintiff has not established a triable issue of material fact as to whether the faded crosswalk line amounts to a dangerous condition, as defined in Govt. Code sec. 830(a). The Court finds that, viewing the evidence most favorably to the plaintiff, the risk created by the faded crosswalk line was of such a minor, trivial, or insignificant nature in view of the surrounding circumstances that no reasonable person could conclude that the condition created a substantial risk of injury when such property or adjacent property was used with due care in a manner in which it was reasonably foreseeable that it would be used. (Gov. Code sec. 830.2.) It is undisputed that the intersection is controlled by traffic lights and pedestrian signals which govern the right of way of all users. (UMF no. 12). In the approximate ten years before the subject accident, there were no reported pedestrian collisions in the crosswalk where Plaintiff was struck. (UMF no. 21.) Plaintiff sets forth no evidence refuting this fact. (See Fielder v. City of Glendale (1977) 71 Cal.App.3d 719, 734 in determining whether a condition is dangerous "the court should see if there is any evidence that other persons have been injured on this same defect.") Further, the pictures of the intersection show that the crosswalk line is faded in parts, but is not "non-existent," as asserted by Plaintiff's expert. (Declaration of William R. Neuman, 6(A)(1), Exs. 3-4; see also Declaration of Brian Woo, Ex. D.) The parts of the first line of the crosswalk that are not faded clearly delineate the existence of the first crosswalk line, which is in the driver's line of sight prior to and during the turn. (Id.)

rulings.Case Title: BRYAN LOGAN VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Motion For Reconsideration
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, June 12, 2014, Line 2, PART II OF TENTATIVE RULING - Plaintiff'S Motion For Reconsideration The Court finds that the partially faded crosswalk line does not create a substantial risk of injury if an individual drives with due care at the signal-controlled intersection. Alternatively, even if Plaintiff could establish that the faded crosswalk line amounted to a dangerous condition, the dangerous condition must be the proximate cause of the injury for liability to arise. (Gov. Code sec. 835.) Veh. Code § 21950 states that a driver of a vehicle "shall yield the right-of-way to a pedestrian crossing the roadway within any marked crosswalk or within any unmarked crosswalk at an intersection¿" Thus, as argued by Defendant, regardless of whether there was a marked crosswalk, the driver still had to yield to crossing pedestrians. The pedestrian crossing signals are lined up with the first crosswalk line; thus the driver should have known she had an obligation to stop for pedestrians. (Woo Decl., Ex. D.) The evidence establishes that the driver's failure to comply with the Vehicle Code proximately caused Plaintiff's injuries. rulings.Case Title: SAMUEL JAYME et al VS. SETON MEDICAL CENTER, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, June 12, 2014, Line 5, DEFENDANTS ATRIUM MEDICAL CORPORATION, MYCHALL MORRIS AND JONATHAN LUNG'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT Sustained in part and overruled in part. As to the first cause of action for Strict Liability, the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. The Second Amended Complaint ("SAC") does not plead that the mesh product was inherently dangerous without instructions. (DeLeon v. Commercial Manufacturing Supply (1983) 148 Cal.App.3d 336, 343.) Further, Plaintiffs cite no authority for the proposition that a medical device manufacturer can be held strictly liable for not training a physician on how to use a device. As to the second cause of action for Negligence, the demurrer is overruled. The SAC pleads that the Defendant medical device representatives "participated" in the "surgical procedure." (SAC, par. 11.) Because Defendants participated in the surgery, Defendants owed a duty to the decedent. The SAC further pleads that Defendants' participation in the surgery "caused decedent's death." (SAC, par. 11.) This sufficiently pleads causation. As to the third cause of action for Right to Privacy, the demurrer is overruled. The SAC adequately pleads that Defendants violated the decedent's right to privacy by being present during the surgery without her consent. (SAC, pars. 11, 27.) rulings.Case Title: LISA ASHBY VS. STRYKER CORPORATION, MCKESSON, A CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, June 12, 2014, Line 10, DEFENDANT STRYKER CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Demurrer is sustained in part and overruled in part. Defendant's Demurrer to the entire Amended Complaint on the ground of uncertainty is overruled. Plaintiff alleges that her injury is attributable to a single product which is sufficiently described in the pleadings. Further, nothing in Bockrath v. Aldrich Chemical Co., Inc., ((1991) 21 Cal. 4th 71) or any other case cited by Defendant establishes that further specificity in the identification of the product-such as a serial or catalogue number-is required. Additionally, each cause of action incorporates Plaintiff's allegations that Stryker is the manufacturer and McKesson is the distributor, thereby giving Defendant notice of the capacity in which each cause of action is brought against it. Defendant's Demurrer to the first cause of action is moot in light of Plaintiff's concession to Defendant's Motion to Strike and the Demurrer to the second cause of action is sustained with ten days leave to amend for Plaintiff to allege facts that identify what aspect of the product make it defective. The Demurrer is sustained without leave to amend as to the fourth cause of action, and is sustained with ten days leave to amend for the fifth cause of action for Plaintiff to allege what statements were made that constituted the express warranty, who made them, and to which defendant they are attributable. Finally, with respect to the sixth, seventh, and eighth causes of action, the Demurrer is sustained with ten days leave to amend for Plaintiff to allege with particularity facts that show how, when, where, to whom, and by what means the representations were tendered in order to satisfy the heightened pleading standard required for fraud claims. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4:00 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if in pro per on the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.
